title: Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow? 8 Causes & Fixes
excerpt: Yellowing monstera leaves are one of the most common plant problems, but the good news is they're almost always fixable. Discover the 8 most likely causes and exactly what to do about them.
content:
Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow? Here's What's Going On

You've been lovingly caring for your monstera, and then one day you notice it — those gorgeous, glossy leaves are starting to look a little… yellow. Don't panic. Yellowing leaves are one of the most common monstera complaints, and in most cases, your plant is simply trying to tell you something. Once you decode the message, the fix is usually straightforward.

In this guide, we'll walk through the eight most likely reasons your monstera is turning yellow, how to diagnose which one applies to your plant, and exactly what steps to take to bring it back to its lush, green best.

1. Overwatering (The Most Common Culprit)

If your monstera's leaves are yellowing — especially if the yellow is spreading across the whole leaf and the soil feels consistently damp — overwatering is the most likely cause. Monstera roots need oxygen, and when they sit in soggy soil for too long, they can't breathe properly. This leads to root rot, which cuts off the plant's ability to absorb nutrients, causing leaves to yellow and eventually die.

What to do:

  • Stick your finger 2 inches into the soil. Only water when the top 2 inches are dry.
  • Check that your pot has pots with drainage holes — this is non-negotiable for monsteras.
  • If you suspect root rot, unpot the plant, trim any black or mushy roots with sterile scissors, and repot in fresh, well-draining soil.

2. Underwatering

Yes, too little water can also cause yellowing — though the pattern looks a little different. Underwatered monsteras often show yellowing that starts at the tips or edges of leaves, and the soil will be bone dry and pulling away from the sides of the pot.

What to do:

  • Water thoroughly until water flows freely from the drainage holes.
  • Establish a consistent watering schedule based on your home's humidity and light levels rather than a fixed number of days.
  • Consider a soil moisture meter if you find it hard to judge when to water.

3. Too Much Direct Sunlight

Monsteras love bright, indirect light — but direct sun, especially through south or west-facing windows in summer, can scorch their leaves. Sun-damaged leaves often turn yellow with brown, crispy patches or bleached-out spots.

What to do:

  • Move your monstera back from the window or use a sheer curtain to diffuse harsh light.
  • Aim for a spot with bright, indirect light for at least 6 hours a day.
  • Once scorched, leaves won't recover, but new growth should come in healthy once the light issue is resolved.

4. Insufficient Light

On the flip side, too little light can also cause yellowing over time. In low-light conditions, your monstera can't photosynthesize efficiently, which slows growth and causes older leaves to turn yellow as the plant redirects its limited energy.

What to do:

  • Move your plant to a brighter spot — ideally within 3-5 feet of a window.
  • If natural light is limited in your space, a grow light used for 10-12 hours a day can make a big difference.

5. Nutrient Deficiencies

Monsteras are relatively hungry plants, and if yours hasn't been fertilized in a while — or is growing in depleted soil — it may start showing yellowing leaves due to a lack of key nutrients, particularly nitrogen, iron, or magnesium.

Nitrogen deficiency usually causes older, lower leaves to yellow first. Iron or magnesium deficiency tends to show up as yellowing between the leaf veins (called interveinal chlorosis), while the veins themselves stay green.

What to do:

  • During the growing season (spring and summer), fertilize your monstera once a month with a balanced, water-soluble balanced liquid fertilizer.
  • If you suspect a specific deficiency, look for a fertilizer that targets that nutrient.
  • Avoid over-fertilizing — too much fertilizer causes salt buildup that can burn roots and also lead to yellowing.

6. Temperature Stress and Cold Drafts

Monsteras are tropical plants that thrive in temperatures between 65–85°F (18–29°C). Cold drafts from air conditioning units, open windows in winter, or placement near exterior doors can cause leaf yellowing and even dark spots or wilting.

What to do:

  • Keep your monstera away from vents, drafty windows, and exterior doors.
  • Avoid placing it near radiators too — sudden heat spikes are equally stressful.
  • Aim for a stable temperature environment with no sudden fluctuations.

7. Pests

Sap-sucking pests like spider mites, mealybugs, and scale insects can cause widespread yellowing as they drain the plant of nutrients. Check the undersides of leaves carefully — you might spot tiny bugs, webbing, sticky residue (called honeydew), or small brown bumps.

What to do:

  • Isolate your plant immediately to prevent the infestation from spreading to other plants.
  • Wipe down leaves with a damp cloth and treat with neem oil or insecticidal soap, repeating every 7-10 days.
  • For severe infestations, a systemic insecticide may be necessary.

For a deeper dive into dealing with common monstera pests, check out our guide on identifying and treating houseplant pests.

8. Natural Aging

Sometimes, yellowing is simply nature doing its thing. It's completely normal for monstera plants to shed their oldest, lowest leaves as they grow. If you're only seeing one or two yellow leaves at a time — always the most mature leaves near the base of the plant — this is likely just natural leaf turnover and nothing to worry about.

What to do:

  • Simply remove yellowing leaves with clean scissors or pruning shears to keep your plant looking tidy and to redirect energy to healthy new growth.
  • If you're seeing more than a couple of yellowing leaves at once, it's worth investigating one of the other causes above.

How to Diagnose Your Monstera's Yellow Leaves

Still not sure which cause applies to your plant? Work through this quick checklist:

  1. Check the soil moisture — is it soggy, dry, or just right?
  2. Look at the pattern — is it the whole leaf, the edges, or between the veins?
  3. Assess the light — how many hours of indirect light does your plant get?
  4. Inspect for pests — check both sides of the leaves carefully.
  5. Think about recent changes — did you move the plant, change your watering routine, or change the season?

The Bottom Line

A yellowing monstera is trying to communicate with you — and once you understand the language, it's much easier to help your plant thrive. In most cases, adjusting your watering habits will solve the problem, but light, nutrients, temperature, and pests are all worth ruling out.

With a little detective work and the right adjustments, your monstera should bounce back and reward you with those beautiful, iconic split leaves. If you're looking to expand your monstera knowledge, explore our guides on monstera propagation, how to get more splits in monstera leaves, and the best soil mix for tropical houseplants.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is my monstera turning yellow?

Yellowing monstera leaves are most commonly caused by overwatering, but can also result from underwatering, too much or too little light, or nutrient deficiencies. Diagnosing the cause requires checking soil moisture, light conditions, and the pattern of yellowing.

How do I fix an overwatered monstera with yellow leaves?

Only water when the top 2 inches of soil are dry, ensure your pot has drainage holes, and if root rot is present, trim black or mushy roots with sterile scissors and repot in fresh well-draining soil.

Can too much sunlight cause monstera leaves to turn yellow?

Yes, direct sunlight can scorch monstera leaves, causing yellowing with brown crispy patches or bleached spots. Move the plant back from the window or use a sheer curtain to provide bright, indirect light instead.

How much light does a monstera need to prevent yellow leaves?

Monsteras need bright, indirect light for at least 6 hours a day, ideally positioned within 3-5 feet of a window. If natural light is limited, a grow light running 10-12 hours daily can compensate.

Why are the tips and edges of my monstera leaves turning yellow?

Yellowing that starts at the tips or edges of leaves typically indicates underwatering. Check if the soil is bone dry and pulling away from the pot sides, then water thoroughly until it drains freely from the drainage holes.
